item.title: Desert Island Disks
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Desert Island Disks

The five songs I can’t live without.

‘Downtown’ - Majical Cloudz

I would be hard pressed to find a song that means quite as much to me as this one. ‘Downtown’ is by far my most listened to track, racking up over two thousand listens since I discovered it just under ten years ago. I was first introduced to the song through one of my favourite shows, Brit Marling’s The OA (2016), which I remember vividly watching over and over in the summer following the end of my GCSE exams. The scene in which ‘Downtown’ features is one which requires a whole red-string board of explanation, so I won’t try to unravel that here.

‘Downtown’ is a melancholy, hypnotic ballad which immediately sunk its claws into me and has refused to budge ever since. It has a rare power that I have not found in any other songs: it has grown with me, capturing my sense of being both at sixteen and twenty-five. This is the song that I show to people when I want them to know me. It is both love and heartbreak, comfort and loneliness.

‘With or Without You’ - U2

I don’t remember the first time I heard U2’s iconic single ‘With Or Without You’. I was probably so young that it passed unnoticed into the background of my life, carrying with it a steady stream of memories into my adult life. Bono is a juggernaut in this song, it’s one of U2’s biggest hits for a reason. The only way I can describe his voice is transcendent. If I listen to this song alone at night I can feel almost as if I’ve cheated death. It’s a beautiful and painful depiction of a troubled relationship. And the melody is just so damn good.

‘My Kind of Woman’ - Mac DeMarco

This is by far the newest addition to the list. I discovered this song only at the start of 2026, a few days before New Year. In the span of a few days of travelling to Norway, I had managed to rack up over 600 listens (it helped that I had the 14-hour journey from hell there and back again). The first chord lassoed me with the grip of a noose and every time I listen it never loses its shine. It is a difficult decision to rate a song so high when I have only known it for half a year, but you know what they say: when you know, you know.

One thing I love about ‘My Kind of Woman’ is the artful panning (stereophonic sound playing different melodies and rhythms in each headphone). I can almost always be found with my trusty (for the eight-month shelf life) wired Apple headphones, and one of my favourite things to do while listening into this song is to focus in turn on the layered elements in each respective headphone. It’s a technique I’ve loved for a while, one which I first grew to adore in a near-inclusion in this list, ‘The Masterplan’ by Oasis. DeMarco somehow manages to capture just about every emotion of the last six months in this one song, from falling in love to falling apart. Even if in years to come this song loses some of its original excitement, it will still doubtlessly bring me right back here, to these months of my life.

‘Read My Mind’ - The Killers

It’s a big ask to name my favourite song by The Killers. I’ve been a fan since I was probably fourteen or fifteen, cemented completely in my years at sixth form college, where I have many a treasured memory of listening to their entire discography between college, home and work. However, it only takes a little relaxation of the brain for the answer to become achingly clear. It’s got to be ‘Read My Mind’, a standout single from their second album, Sam’s Town. While at a push, I would probably choose Hot Fuss for a desert island album, ‘Read My Mind’ stands out to be as one of the greatest Killers songs and one which has the most emotional pull in my life.

It’s the bridge that really captures me. I remember being obsessed with it while writing my first full-length novel (which has since been shelved and will likely never see the light of day). I love the music video, which features the band members roving around Tokyo. I love the instrumentals, I love the lyrics (‘slippin’ in my faith until I fall’ is simply brilliant), I love the beautiful sense of scale and the Springsteen-esque vibe. It’s simply a classic to me.

‘No 1 Party Anthem’ - Arctic Monkeys

Although these aren’t ranked in order, I think it’s fitting to round off this list with Arctic Monkeys’ ‘No 1 Party Anthem’. It’s the last song at the party, it’s collecting the rubbish at the end of the night and wondering where it all went wrong. This song could make me cry. It’s haunting in more ways than one, and there are years of my life that are lost within its hypnotic melody. It’s not a love song, rather far from it, which is once again fitting for the period of my life I associate it with.

Again, the bridge seals the deal. It is the feeling of looking across the room at someone and knowing you have to let them go. In my opinion, it’s AM at their finest, no notes. I’ve spent many an afternoon perfecting a music video for this song in my mind, daydreaming about an opportunity to direct it for myself one day. Increasingly it looks unlikely, but that doesn’t stop me entertaining it. It’s a good way to round off this list, the moment it ends you think ‘yeah, time to head home.’

item.title: Laverstock Hill in Bloom
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Laverstock Hill in Bloom

I spent many afternoons during my school years up Laverstock Hill. Although I have not visited in a while, it is still warmly wrapped up in nostalgia, the soft blanket of childhood memory. I have videos and photographs of rolling down the grassy dunes of the field behind my school, of the herd of cows that roved intermittently across the downs, of the swing fashioned from old rope and a plank of wood that sat overlooking the city. In PE, they made us run up and back down the hill for cross country. The yearly ‘mini marathon’ was a mile-round trip of the muddy, trodden paths up to the hill. It has been a while since I’ve done that walk, years, but I can still remember the route like the back of my hand, could do it blindfolded, even. In those memories, I recall a very straightforward time, my life regimented by timetables and lunches and GCSE exams. Yet, they were also the careless years, the years before pretending, when I was old enough to know who I was but still young enough not to question it. Who could hold their handstand for the longest? How do I get out of this year’s Sports Day? Whose house for a sleepover?

These photos are from the last time I walked the route in its entirety, in June of 2020, the peak of Britain’s COVID-19 lockdown. I walked it alone, listened to a few good songs, walked almost directly in the footsteps of myself at fifteen, twelve, seven. It’s still as beautiful as it was then. I start with ‘Wildfire’ by Seafret and end with ‘Getaway’ by Blossoms. These songs too are relics now, almost six years since they were on repeat in all my playlists. I used to have a habit of taking photos of my screen with my Lumix bridge camera, an ‘artsy’ way of flexing my music taste to my 63 followers on Instagram. While writing this, I thought to search Laverstock Hill up online, expecting perhaps a description of the nature or geological structure, but I found nothing. A short article from the Salisbury Journal recounting a similar experience of walking up and viewing the city from the ‘summit’, written in 2010. Laverstock Hill is not a spectacular formation, it is not a noteworthy landscape, and yet to me it has inscribed so many years of my life that it may as well be as important as Ben Nevis or Kilimanjaro. Of course, I jest. But then again, maybe I don’t. We leave ourselves all over the landscape; a place is never just a place.

item.title: (Still) Life
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
(Still) Life

I have never been the biggest fan of still life paintings. In my “local” museum, Cambridge’s Fitzwilliam, the first room in the upstairs gallery is a self-proclaimed “Flower Room”, the walls lined from floor to ceiling with technically impeccable depictions of flower vases. I have always liked this room, largely for the overall impression it gives, the way you can lose yourself in the uniformity and simplicity. Take a closer look, however, and I’m almost immediately bored. All the paintings look the same. While their technique and skill are faultless, these painting don’t make me feel very much, and what’s the point of art if it doesn’t make you feel, right?

We painted a lot of still life in school for this very purpose: it develops important skills within a controlled environment, that allows an artist to develop their understanding of several important aspects of painting. As the artist, you set up the composition, meaning that you decide on the light, the objects, the difficulty, as well as creating through the image a narrative, theme or colour harmony. Still life is not a simple snapshot of some objects lying around, it is often carefully orchestrated, it can mean something just as a portrait might. Some still life paintings worth mentioning are Paul Cezanne’s Still Life with Sugar, Caravaggio’s Basket of Fruit, Vincent Van Gogh’s Sunflowers and John Singer Sargent’s Roses. I’ll focus on each in turn. Cezanne’s Still Life carries with it a distinct sense of movement beyond the frame, the tablecloth is rumpled beneath the fruit, the scene feels rushed and quite technically inaccurate in terms of its use of perspective. It is an impression fit for a (post)impressionist. Caravaggio is, in contrast, far more technically accurate. His level of detail is immaculate, if a little harshly defined against the plain background. This reminds me of the flower paintings, although I do feel a twinge of something looking at it, an admiration of the Baroque perfection, perhaps. Sunflowers needs no further introduction. If you were ever to feel something looking at a still life, you would feel it with this. What makes Sunflowers so compelling is not just the fact that we know the artist (as Van Gogh’s own life and history are doubtlessly a powerful emotional pull to his paintings), but it is that we feel as if we truly know the subject. When I look at this painting, I can see the moment it’s captured, the flowers that will doubtlessly shrivel and die beyond the frame, engendering a melancholy beauty to their proud heads. Sargent’s Roses are just a step beyond. The beautifully composed handful of roses lie on the edge of a desk, they are almost decaying before our eyes.

There, then, is the beauty I have found in still life. For a moment, they stop time, they stop decay, they stop life. They exist in a moment where the flower is forever in bloom, the plum always ripe, the apple always browning. All those flowers in the Fitzwilliam died a very long time ago, and every time a curious visitor steps into Gallery 17, they are born again.

The paintings throughout this article are attempts of still life from a holiday in Wales in 2025. They are not technically admirable, rely far more on bold colour and contrast than they do on meaning or vision. They are painted with gouache, each completed within a two-hour period (the time it takes to watch a film) and inspired not by objects before me, but rather hastily found Pinterest pics. They did, however, give me a little taste of still life splendour, provided a drive to try more, to compose my own images, and to depict life even when it’s standing still.

item.title: Black Swan(s)
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Black Swans

2021, the second year of my undergraduate degree at the University of Exeter. My house is at the very end of the highstreet, almost overlooking the River Exe. If you follow the water, you will reach the quay, bracketed with craft stalls built into the landscape, restaurants and bridges and benches that in their reliable architecture hold (what felt at the time) my entire life and identity. I have recently been gifted my first DSLR, an upgrade from a bridge camera I dutifully and loyally kept safe since I was thirteen years old. I walk up and down the quay every day, often twice a day, itching for company in the tense post-lockdown months, resigned to obsessive work at my rickety wooden desk and these brief, beautiful snatches of sun in the early morning.

Black swans (cygnus atratus) are rare in the UK. In the Merlin app (my trusty ornithology buddy), it is a solid red circle (rare) and sighting one in any given area of the UK is classified as “unlikely”. Black swans are native to southeast and southwest Australia, where they gravitate to both fresh and saltwater lakes, swamps, rivers and wetlands. They are nomadic, with varying migration patterns. In terms of appearance, they are large, black-feathered birds with white flight feathers and vibrant red bills, a bright splash that would draw any wandering eye on an afternoon stroll. Across the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, they were introduced as ornamental birds in New Zealand, western Europe and Japan, with limited numbers in the US. Aside from Australia, however, their most prominent geographical association is in Dawlish, Devon. In fact, they are an established emblem of the town [https://ladysmile.co.uk/blogs/black-swans-of-dawlish-the-history-and-influence]. As such, on one routine stroll down the River Exe, I was met with a mating pair (we all deserve a holiday, right?).

After a mad dash back to my house to collect my camera, I spent at least an hour as a desperate paparazzi. Now, they remain the rarest birds (or, I would say, animal in general) that I have witnessed in the wild. I look at these pictures, and I am met not only with the beauty and majesty of their great, streamlined bodies, but a more baseline affection for both Devon and those years of mine in Exeter. I spent a lot of time photographing birds, attracted, I think, by their apparent freedom of movement. But introduction of non-native species within human-led currents is hardly freedom of movement, is it? I stood on the bank of the river and watched these birds and felt a great and affectionate calling. “We are both a little far from home, are we not?”

I had never seen black swans before, and I have not seen them since. I left Exeter and I rarely look back. However, sometimes I am looking through the thousands of photos I have taken over my life and I find these again, and for a moment I can see the crowd of them, the bright flash of red and the distinctive white stripe, and I am back there on the bank, crouching down, close enough to photograph but not to risk provoking them. Two animals facing each other off. One wild and one tame.

item.title: Stave Churches in Norway
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Stave Churches in Norway

In January of 2024, I travelled to Norway to stay at my friend’s cabin, and ski for the first time. We met while she was studying abroad in London and have since taken turns to show each other some of the dear and wonderful parts of our respective countries. We stayed a few hours from Oslo, and drove back in the chilling winter snow, my toes perpetually numb, every moment outside marked by the feeling of frost coating the inside of my nostrils. We saw great stone trolls, sweeping snowy roads, dense fir forests, and through the blanket of white, the unique, awesome spires of Norway’s surviving stave churches. Only 28 stave churches remain standing in Norway, constructed from wood, largely built between 1150 and 1350. They are common in high valleys and forest land, where the areas are less populated. While hundreds of stave churches were originally built, the majority have been destroyed through redesign, conversion, dismantlement or natural disasters like fires, storms or avalanches.

We couldn’t stay outside for long. A true Brit, even my warmest clothes were unequipped for the Norway cold. However, these great and beautiful buildings stick in my mind. I am always fond of a spire, tied as I am to the welcoming beacon of home that is the spire of Salisbury cathedral, the rich maroon against the stark white snow, the peaking headstones, the elegant and neat wood. On the same drive, we saw a moose. I have never been to Vermont, but I listen to Noah Kahan every time I set food in Norway, and when I get home, I’m always Homesick for a country that’s not my own.

item.title: Elan Valley
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Elan Valley

In March of 2025, I visited Wales for a few nights with one of my closest friends. We had discovered a dream BnB a year before while attending the Hay Festival at Hay-on-Wye: a love letter to the world’s unapologetic book nerds. Both employed, rolling in our minimally disposable cash, we planned a trip of beautiful breakfasts, gentle strolls through the countryside and a few tasteful hours lying in bed watching every Night at the Museum. The highlight of our trip however, and almost entirely unexpected, was the six hours we got totally lost while “hiking” the stunning Elan Valley.

We started with a reasonable stroll through an established path, where I was stuck with boundless enthusiasm to the “record” function of the Merlin app. Itching for a little more adventure, we went off path, encouraged by passers-by that the loop lasted only an hour or two, and that we were, seemingly at every point “nearly there”. We had no water, no snacks (travesty!) and no signal, so for the following six hours, we were wandering about in the hope that we were travelling somewhat in the right direction of the car park. I do have to confess that my survival skills are minimal (something that I plan to rectify in the coming years) and there was one stretch of A-road with no signal that almost sent me spiralling. We met gaggles of watchful rams and a group of four boys who led us through (our Hobbit savours), a red kite and some of the most beautiful sights I have seen in Britain. I remember often the farm we passed that carried such a malevolent energy that the crazed barking still visits me in my sleep. I often have dreams that involve farms and fearful feeling. One, which I must have had when I was fifteen or younger, was simply a soundtrack of squealing pigs being slaughtered. Anyway, I digress.

Back to civilisation, we got a great curry and slathered our blisters in antiseptic. Elan Valley remains a very fond memory, for the spontaneous “hike”, the hilarious antics, and even the inconsistent but imposing sense of dread. I would certainly go back at the earliest opportunity. I would even get lost again. Some better shoes and a packet of biscuits, then send me on my way.
